7.1.1.intuition: Think of the OWASP Top 10 as the most common ways a burglar can break into a house. Broken access control is like having locks that any key can open. Injection is like a mailbox slot big enough to reach in and unlock the door from inside. Security misconfiguration is like leaving a window wide open with a sign saying 'come in.'

exercises.title: Security Header Audit
exercises.description: Create an Express application with helmet configured. Use curl or a browser's developer tools to inspect the response headers. Document each security header that helmet sets, what attack it prevents, and what the default value is. Then customize helmet to add a strict Content-Security-Policy that only allows scripts from your own domain.
exercises.hint: Use `curl -I http://localhost:3000` to see headers. Check helmet's documentation for the CSP directives: `helmet({ contentSecurityPolicy: { directives: { ... } } })`.

exercises.title: Build a Rate Limiter Middleware
exercises.description: Implement a custom rate limiting middleware from scratch (without express-rate-limit) that uses an in-memory Map to track request counts per IP address. It should allow 10 requests per minute, return 429 when exceeded, and include a Retry-After header. Add a cleanup function that removes expired entries every 5 minutes.
exercises.hint: Use a Map<string, { count: number, resetTime: number }>. On each request, check if the IP exists and whether the window has expired. Use setInterval for cleanup.

exercises.title: Dependency Vulnerability Report
exercises.description: Take any Node.js project with dependencies. Run `npm audit --json`, parse the output, and create a summary report that groups vulnerabilities by severity (critical, high, moderate, low), lists the affected packages, and identifies which ones are direct vs transitive dependencies.
exercises.hint: Pipe `npm audit --json` to a script or use JSON.parse on the output. The audit JSON has a `vulnerabilities` object keyed by package name with severity and dependency path information.

exercises.title: Secure Error Handler
exercises.description: Write an Express error handling middleware that: (1) logs the full error with stack trace to a file using Winston, (2) returns a sanitized error to the client with no stack trace in production, (3) includes a unique error ID in both the log and the response so support can correlate user reports with server logs, and (4) handles known error types (ValidationError, AuthenticationError, NotFoundError) differently.
exercises.hint: Generate an error ID with `crypto.randomUUID()`. Create custom error classes that extend Error with a statusCode property. Switch on `err.constructor.name` or use instanceof checks.

problems.problem: Application leaks stack traces and internal paths in production error responses
problems.cause: The default Express error handler or a custom error handler is not checking NODE_ENV and sends err.stack to the client. Attackers use this information to map your file structure and identify framework versions.
problems.solution: Always check `process.env.NODE_ENV` in your error handler. In production, return only a generic message and an error correlation ID. Log the full error server-side. Use `app.disable('x-powered-by')` to prevent framework fingerprinting.

problems.problem: npm audit reports dozens of vulnerabilities but npm audit fix does not resolve them
problems.cause: Most unresolvable audit findings come from transitive (indirect) dependencies where the direct dependency has not released a fix yet. Some are also false positives for your usage.
problems.solution: Use `npm audit --json` to identify which direct dependencies pull in the vulnerable packages. Check if newer major versions of the direct dependency fix the issue. Use the `overrides` field in package.json to force a patched version of the transitive dependency. For false positives, document the finding and add it to an allow-list in your CI pipeline.

problems.problem: CORS errors block requests from the frontend in development, so someone sets origin to '*' and it gets shipped to production
problems.cause: Wildcard CORS (`*`) allows any website to make requests to your API, which is dangerous for APIs that use cookies or authentication. It often gets set during development and forgotten.
problems.solution: Use environment-specific CORS configuration. In development, allow localhost origins. In production, set an explicit allowlist. Store allowed origins in environment variables: `cors({ origin: process.env.ALLOWED_ORIGINS.split(',') })`. Add a CI check that verifies CORS is not set to '*' in production config files.

further_reading.note: The definitive reference for the top 10 web application security risks with detailed descriptions and prevention guidance

further_reading.note: An intentionally vulnerable Node.js app designed to teach OWASP Top 10 risks with hands-on exercises

further_reading.note: Security section of the comprehensive Node.js best practices guide with production-tested recommendations

further_reading.note: Documentation for the helmet middleware that sets various HTTP security headers

7.1.2.intuition: Imagine a bank teller who reads instructions from a slip of paper the customer fills out. SQL injection is like a customer writing 'withdraw $100 from account 123 and also transfer all money from every account to account 999' on the slip, and the teller blindly following every instruction. Parameterized queries are like giving the teller a pre-printed form with fixed instructions and blank fields -- no matter what the customer writes in the blanks, the teller only performs the pre-defined action.

exercises.title: Identify the Injection Points
exercises.hint: There are 5 injection points: startDate, endDate, department (use $1, $2, $3), sortBy (must be whitelisted since column names cannot be parameterized), and limit (parameterize as integer). Add Zod validation for all inputs.

exercises.title: Build a Safe Dynamic Query Builder
exercises.description: Write a function `buildProductQuery(filters)` that accepts an object with optional fields (search, category, minPrice, maxPrice, inStock, sortBy, order) and returns a { text, values } object for use with pool.query(). The function must: (1) use parameterized queries for all data values, (2) whitelist sortBy and order, (3) dynamically build the WHERE clause only for provided filters, and (4) handle the LIKE operator safely.
exercises.hint: Track a parameter counter (let paramIndex = 1) and push to a values array as you build conditions. Increment paramIndex each time you add a parameter. For LIKE, put the % in the value, not the query: `name ILIKE $${paramIndex}` with value `%${search}%`.

exercises.title: NoSQL Injection Prevention
exercises.description: Create a login endpoint for a MongoDB-based Express app that is resistant to NoSQL injection. The endpoint should: (1) validate that username and password are strings (not objects), (2) use bcrypt for password comparison (not database-level comparison), (3) return consistent error messages to prevent user enumeration, and (4) include a test suite with injection payloads like `{"$gt": ""}` and `{"$ne": null}`.
exercises.hint: Never pass user input directly to MongoDB's find() without type checking. Fetch the user by username (validated as string), then compare the password in application code with bcrypt.compare().

problems.problem: ORM raw query methods bypass built-in parameterization
problems.cause: Developers use ORM escape hatches like Prisma's $queryRawUnsafe, Knex's knex.raw() with string interpolation, or Sequelize's sequelize.query() with template literals instead of parameter bindings. These raw methods do not auto-parameterize.
problems.solution: For Prisma, always use `$queryRaw` with tagged template literals (not `$queryRawUnsafe`). For Knex, always pass a bindings array as the second argument to `knex.raw('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id])`. For Sequelize, use replacements: `sequelize.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :id', { replacements: { id } })`. Audit your codebase with grep for `queryRawUnsafe`, `knex.raw` without bindings, and template literals inside `.query()` calls.

problems.problem: Parameterized queries do not work for ORDER BY, table names, or column names
problems.cause: SQL parameter placeholders ($1, ?) can only be used for data values, not SQL identifiers (table names, column names) or keywords (ASC/DESC). Databases interpret parameters as literal strings in those positions, causing syntax errors.
problems.solution: Maintain a strict whitelist of allowed column names and sort directions. Validate user input against the whitelist before inserting into the query string. Example: `const ALLOWED = ['name','price','created_at']; if (!ALLOWED.includes(sortBy)) throw new Error('Invalid sort');`. Never use user input directly for identifiers, even if you think you have escaped it.

problems.problem: LIKE queries with user input return unexpected results or are slow
problems.cause: User input containing SQL wildcard characters (% and _) causes unexpected pattern matching. Input like '%' matches everything, and complex patterns can cause slow sequential scans.
problems.solution: Escape wildcard characters in user input before using them in LIKE parameters: `const escaped = input.replace(/%/g, '\\%').replace(/_/g, '\\_');`. Then use: `WHERE name LIKE $1` with value `%${escaped}%`. For performance, consider PostgreSQL full-text search (`tsvector`/`tsquery`) or a dedicated search engine (Elasticsearch) instead of LIKE for user-facing search.

further_reading.title: OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet
further_reading.note: Comprehensive prevention guide covering parameterized queries, stored procedures, whitelist validation, and escaping across all languages

further_reading.title: PortSwigger SQL Injection Tutorial
further_reading.note: Interactive labs and detailed explanations of all SQL injection types including blind, UNION, and second-order injection

further_reading.title: node-postgres Parameterized Queries Documentation
further_reading.note: Official documentation for parameterized queries in node-postgres (pg), the most popular PostgreSQL client for Node.js

further_reading.title: Prisma Security Best Practices
further_reading.note: Prisma's guide on using raw SQL safely, explaining the difference between $queryRaw (safe) and $queryRawUnsafe (dangerous)

7.1.3.intuition: XSS is like a ventriloquist who tricks the website into saying things it never intended, making the victim's browser believe the malicious script is from the trusted site. CSRF is like someone forging your signature on a bank check -- the bank recognizes your signature (cookie) and processes the transaction without realizing you did not write the check. SSRF is like tricking an employee into using their badge to access restricted areas on your behalf.

exercises.title: Build an XSS-Proof Comment System
exercises.description:
Build a complete comment API with POST /api/comments (create) and GET /api/comments (list) endpoints. The system should: (1) accept rich text input (bold, italic, links only), (2) strip all dangerous tags (script, iframe, img with onerror, event handlers), (3) use DOMPurify for sanitization, (4) set proper CSP headers, and (5) include tests with XSS payloads including ``, ``, `click`, and `
hover
`.
exercises.hint: Install dompurify and jsdom. Configure DOMPurify with ALLOWED_TAGS: ['b','i','em','strong','a','p','br'] and ALLOWED_ATTR: ['href']. For the link XSS, DOMPurify removes javascript: URIs by default.

exercises.title: Implement Double-Submit CSRF Protection
exercises.description: Implement CSRF protection from scratch using the double-submit cookie pattern. Create: (1) a GET /api/csrf-token endpoint that generates a random token, stores it in a cookie and returns it in the response body, (2) a middleware that validates the token from the X-CSRF-Token header matches the cookie value on state-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE), (3) tests that verify requests without tokens are rejected and requests with mismatched tokens are rejected.
exercises.hint: Use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') for token generation. Set the cookie with httpOnly: false so the client JS can read it. The middleware should compare req.cookies['csrf-token'] with req.headers['x-csrf-token'].

exercises.title: Build an SSRF-Safe URL Preview Service
exercises.description: Build a POST /api/url-preview endpoint that fetches a user-provided URL and returns its title and description. The service must: (1) validate the URL scheme is http or https, (2) resolve DNS and block requests to private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, 169.254.x), (3) handle redirect chains by validating each redirect target, (4) set a timeout of 5 seconds, (5) limit response body size to 1MB. Include test cases for all blocked IP ranges and redirect-based bypasses.
exercises.hint: Use dns.resolve4() from node:dns/promises to get the IP before making the request. For redirect handling, use `redirect: 'manual'` in fetch options and check the Location header. For body size limiting, check the Content-Length header and use a streaming approach with AbortController.

exercises.title: Security Headers Audit Tool
exercises.description: Write a Node.js script that takes a URL as a command-line argument, makes a GET request, and audits the response for security headers. Check for: Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy. For each header, print whether it is present, its value, and rate it as 'good', 'weak', or 'missing'. Output a final security score out of 100.
exercises.hint: Use fetch() to make the request. For rating, check specific values: CSP should not have 'unsafe-inline' or 'unsafe-eval' in script-src. HSTS should have max-age of at least 31536000. X-Frame-Options should be DENY or SAMEORIGIN.

problems.problem: CSP header blocks legitimate inline scripts and styles after enabling helmet
problems.cause: Content-Security-Policy with default settings blocks all inline scripts (`<script>` tags with inline code) and inline styles. Many applications rely on inline scripts for analytics, configuration, or styling, which break immediately.
problems.solution: Use nonces or hashes for legitimate inline scripts instead of 'unsafe-inline'. Generate a unique nonce per request: `const nonce = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString('base64');`, add it to CSP: `script-src 'self' 'nonce-${nonce}'`, and include it on your script tags: `<script nonce="${nonce}">`. For styles, 'unsafe-inline' is generally acceptable as CSS injection is lower risk than script injection. Roll out CSP in report-only mode first: `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only` to identify violations without breaking functionality.

problems.problem: SSRF protection is bypassed using DNS rebinding or URL encoding tricks
problems.cause: The application validates the hostname or IP at request time, but the DNS lookup returns a different IP when the actual HTTP connection is made. Alternatively, attackers use URL encoding (e.g., `http://0x7f000001/` for 127.0.0.1), decimal IP notation (e.g., `http://2130706433/`), or IPv6 representations to bypass naive IP checks.
problems.solution: Resolve DNS once and connect directly to the resolved IP. Block all IPv6 addresses unless you have specific validation for them. Parse and normalize IP addresses from decimal, hex, and octal representations. Use an egress proxy at the network level as defense in depth. Consider a library like `ssrf-req-filter` that handles these edge cases.

problems.problem: SameSite=Strict cookies break OAuth login flows and cross-site redirects
problems.cause: SameSite=Strict prevents the cookie from being sent on ANY cross-site navigation, including when a user clicks a link from an email, returns from an OAuth provider, or is redirected from a payment gateway. The user appears logged out upon arrival.
problems.solution: Use SameSite=Lax instead of Strict for session cookies. Lax allows cookies on top-level GET navigations (clicking links, redirects) but blocks them on cross-site POST requests, which is sufficient to prevent CSRF. For APIs that need Strict protection, use a separate API token in the Authorization header instead of relying on cookies.

further_reading.note: Comprehensive guide covering all XSS contexts (HTML, JavaScript, URL, CSS) with specific encoding rules for each

further_reading.note: Interactive labs covering basic SSRF, blind SSRF, bypassing filters, and SSRF with out-of-band techniques

further_reading.note: Quick reference for all CSP directives with examples and browser compatibility information

further_reading.title: SameSite Cookie Attribute Explained
further_reading.note: Google's clear explanation of SameSite cookie behavior with visual diagrams and practical guidance

7.2.1.intuition: OAuth2 is like a valet parking system. You (the resource owner) give the valet (the client app) a special ticket (access token) that only lets them park and retrieve your car, but they never get a copy of your house keys (password). The Authorization Code flow adds a layer: the valet first gets a claim ticket (authorization code) from the parking desk, then exchanges it at a secure back office window (server-to-server) for the actual car key.

exercises.title: Build a GitHub OAuth2 Login
exercises.description: Register a GitHub OAuth App, then build an Express server that implements the full Authorization Code flow. The user should be able to click 'Login with GitHub', get redirected, and see their GitHub profile information on a dashboard page. Include the state parameter for CSRF protection.
exercises.hint: Register at https://github.com/settings/developers. Set the callback URL to http://localhost:3000/auth/github/callback. Use express-session to store the state parameter and tokens.

exercises.title: Implement PKCE from Scratch
exercises.description: Write a module that generates a code_verifier (43-128 character random string) and derives the code_challenge using SHA-256. Write tests that verify: (1) the verifier is the right length, (2) the challenge is a valid base64url string, (3) the same verifier always produces the same challenge, (4) different verifiers produce different challenges.
exercises.hint: Use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('base64url') for the verifier. Use crypto.createHash('sha256').update(verifier).digest('base64url') for the challenge.

exercises.title: Client Credentials Token Cache
exercises.description: Build a class that implements the Client Credentials flow with automatic token caching and refresh. It should request a new token only when the cached one is expired or about to expire (within 60 seconds of expiry). Write tests using a mock token endpoint.
exercises.hint: Store the token and its expiry time. Before each API call, check if the token is still valid. Use Date.now() + (expires_in * 1000) to calculate expiry.

exercises.title: Token Refresh Middleware
exercises.description: Write an Express middleware that checks if the user's access token is about to expire and transparently refreshes it using the refresh token before the request handler runs. If the refresh fails, redirect the user to the login page.
exercises.hint: Store tokenExpiry in the session. In the middleware, compare it against Date.now(). If within 5 minutes of expiry, call the token endpoint with grant_type=refresh_token.

problems.problem: State parameter mismatch error on OAuth callback
problems.cause: The session storing the state value was lost between the redirect and the callback. This commonly happens when: (1) the session cookie is not being set due to SameSite or Secure attributes, (2) a load balancer routes the callback to a different server instance without shared sessions, or (3) the user took too long and the session expired.
problems.solution: Ensure your session store is shared across instances (use Redis or a database-backed session store). Check that your session cookie settings are correct for your domain. For local development, set `cookie.secure = false`. For production behind a proxy, set `app.set('trust proxy', 1)` and use secure cookies.

problems.problem: Authorization code exchange returns 'invalid_grant' error
problems.cause: The authorization code has already been used (codes are single-use), the code has expired (typically 10 minutes), or the redirect_uri in the token request does not exactly match the one used in the authorization request.
problems.solution: Ensure you exchange the code immediately upon receiving it. Verify the redirect_uri is identical in both requests (including trailing slashes). Do not retry the code exchange if it fails; redirect the user to start the flow again.

problems.problem: Access token works locally but fails in production with CORS errors
problems.cause: When making API calls from the browser, CORS policies block the request. Tokens in the Authorization header trigger a preflight OPTIONS request that the API server may not handle.
problems.solution: For server-side OAuth flows, make API calls from your backend server, not from the browser. If you must call from the browser, ensure the resource server has proper CORS headers. Better yet, create a proxy endpoint on your server that adds the token and forwards the request.

7.2.2.intuition: If OAuth2 is a valet parking ticket that says what the valet can do with your car, then OpenID Connect is a driver's license taped to that ticket. The driver's license (ID token) tells you exactly who the person is, and because it is cryptographically signed by a trusted authority (the identity provider), you can verify it is genuine without calling the DMV every time.

exercises.title: OIDC Discovery Explorer
exercises.description: Write a Node.js script that fetches and displays the OIDC discovery document from three different providers (Google, Microsoft, and an Auth0 tenant). Compare the endpoints and supported features. Output a table showing which provider supports which response types, scopes, and signing algorithms.
exercises.hint: Fetch from {issuer}/.well-known/openid-configuration. Google's issuer is https://accounts.google.com, Microsoft is https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/v2.0.

exercises.title: ID Token Validator
exercises.description: Build a standalone ID token validation module that: (1) fetches the provider's JWKS, (2) finds the correct key by 'kid', (3) verifies the JWT signature, (4) checks iss, aud, exp, iat, and nonce. Write tests with a self-signed JWT to verify each validation step catches invalid tokens.
exercises.hint: Use the jsonwebtoken and jwks-rsa packages. For testing, generate your own RSA key pair with crypto.generateKeyPairSync and sign test JWTs.

exercises.title: Multi-Provider Login Page
exercises.description: Build an Express application that supports login with at least two OIDC providers (e.g., Google and GitHub). Implement account linking so that if a user logs in with Google and later with GitHub using the same email, both identities are connected to one user record in a SQLite database.
exercises.hint: Use a provider registry pattern. Create a 'users' table and an 'identities' table (provider, provider_id, user_id). Check email matches on new identity creation.

problems.problem: ID token validation fails with 'invalid signature' even though the token looks correct
problems.cause: The JWKS keys have been rotated by the provider but your application is using a cached old key. Or you are using the wrong key because the 'kid' (Key ID) in the token header does not match any cached key.
problems.solution: Ensure your JWKS client has a cache expiry (10-15 minutes). When a 'kid' is not found in cache, fetch the JWKS again. The jwks-rsa library handles this with the `cache` and `cacheMaxAge` options. Also verify you are fetching keys from the correct JWKS URI for your provider.

problems.problem: User gets a different 'sub' claim when logging into staging vs production
problems.cause: Each OAuth2 client registration (client_id) at the provider may receive a different 'sub' value for the same user, depending on the provider's policy. Or you are hitting different provider environments (sandbox vs production).
problems.solution: Use the same client_id across environments where possible, or use email (if verified) as the stable identifier for linking. Document your provider's 'sub' stability guarantees. Some providers guarantee sub is stable per client, others per tenant.

problems.problem: Account linking creates duplicate accounts for the same person
problems.cause: The user logged in with Google (email: alice@gmail.com) and later with GitHub (email: alice@gmail.com) but the linking logic did not match them because the GitHub email was not verified, or the emails were compared case-sensitively.
problems.solution: Normalize emails to lowercase before comparison. Only auto-link when email_verified is true from the provider. For unverified emails, prompt the user to manually link accounts by logging into both providers in the same session. Add a unique constraint on the email column.

further_reading.note: The official OIDC specification. Section 2 (ID Token) and Section 3 (Authentication) are the most important.

7.2.3.intuition: RBAC is like a building access card system: your card says you are an "Engineer" and that gives you access to the engineering floor, the lab, and the cafeteria. ABAC is like a smart building that also checks the time of day, whether you booked the conference room, and whether you have completed your safety training before letting you into the lab. Most real buildings use both: the card gets you in the door (RBAC), but certain rooms have additional rules (ABAC).

exercises.title: Build an RBAC System from Scratch
exercises.description: Create a complete RBAC system with an Express API for managing roles, permissions, and user-role assignments. Include endpoints to: create roles, create permissions, assign permissions to roles, assign roles to users, and check if a user has a specific permission. Use SQLite with Knex for storage.
exercises.hint: Create four tables: users, roles, permissions, user_roles, role_permissions. Build a service class that queries across the join tables. Add REST endpoints under /api/rbac/.

exercises.title: ABAC Policy Engine
exercises.description: Build a policy engine that evaluates authorization decisions based on subject, resource, action, and environment attributes. Define at least 5 policies including: ownership-based access, time-based restrictions, and deny-overrides. Write comprehensive tests for each policy.
exercises.hint: Structure policies as objects with a condition function that receives the full context. Process deny policies before allow policies. Return the policy name in the decision for audit logging.

exercises.title: Hybrid RBAC + ABAC Blog Authorization
exercises.description: Build a blog API where: admins can do everything, editors can create/edit their own posts or any draft, viewers can only read published posts, and no one can delete a post that has more than 10 comments. Implement this using RBAC for base permissions and ABAC for contextual rules.
exercises.hint: Use RBAC to check if the user's role has the base permission (e.g., 'posts:delete'). Then use ABAC to check the additional context (e.g., comment count < 10). Both checks must pass.

exercises.title: Permission-Aware API Responses
exercises.description: Extend a REST API so that each resource response includes a 'permissions' object showing what the current user can do (canEdit, canDelete, canPublish). Write middleware that automatically calculates this for any resource type.
exercises.hint: Create a permissions resolver that takes the user and resource, runs them through both RBAC and ABAC checks for each action, and returns a boolean map. Attach it to the response serializer.

problems.problem: User reports they cannot access a resource despite having the correct role
problems.cause: The permission cache is stale. The user was recently assigned a new role but the cached permissions still reflect their old roles. Or there is a typo in the resource/action string (e.g., 'post' vs 'posts').
problems.solution: Invalidate the permission cache whenever roles or permissions change. Use consistent naming conventions for resources and actions. Add logging to the authorization middleware that shows exactly which permission was checked and what the user's effective permissions are.

problems.problem: Role explosion: the system has 50+ roles and they are hard to manage
problems.cause: The team created very specific roles like 'marketing-blog-editor' and 'engineering-blog-editor' instead of using attributes. This happens when RBAC is stretched beyond its design limits.
problems.solution: Migrate to a hybrid RBAC + ABAC model. Reduce roles to broad categories (admin, editor, viewer) and use ABAC policies for fine-grained rules based on department, ownership, or resource attributes. Consider using a library like CASL to define abilities declaratively.

problems.problem: Authorization check passes but the user sees a 403 on some resources and not others
problems.cause: The ABAC policy depends on resource attributes (like ownership or status) that differ between resources. The permission check works for resources the user owns but fails for others, and the error message does not explain which attribute caused the denial.
problems.solution: Add detailed logging to your policy engine that records which policy matched and which condition failed. Return specific error messages like 'You can only edit your own posts' instead of generic 'Forbidden'. Implement a 'dry run' mode that explains why access was denied.

further_reading.title: CASL - Isomorphic Authorization for JavaScript
further_reading.note: The most popular ABAC library for Node.js and JavaScript. Use this in production instead of building from scratch.

further_reading.title: NIST ABAC Guide (SP 800-162)
further_reading.note: The official NIST guide to Attribute Based Access Control. Dry but comprehensive and authoritative.

further_reading.title: Oso - Authorization Academy
further_reading.note: Free course covering authorization patterns from ACLs to ReBAC with practical examples

further_reading.title: Casbin - Authorization Library
further_reading.note: Supports ACL, RBAC, ABAC, and custom models. Has a Node.js adapter and works with multiple databases.

7.3.1.intuition: Password hashing is like a meat grinder: you can put a steak in and get ground meat out, but you cannot reassemble the steak from the ground meat. bcrypt and argon2 are intentionally slow grinders -- they take hundreds of milliseconds on purpose -- so that an attacker who steals your ground meat cannot try billions of steaks per second to find a match.

exercises.title: Password Hashing Benchmark
exercises.description: Write a script that benchmarks bcrypt hashing at cost factors 8 through 16. For each cost, hash the string 'benchmark-test' and record the elapsed time in milliseconds. Print a table showing cost factor vs. time. Determine which cost factor results in approximately 250ms on your machine.
exercises.hint: Use Date.now() before and after each bcrypt.hash() call. Run each cost factor three times and average the results for more accurate measurements.

exercises.title: User Registration and Login Service
exercises.description: Build a complete PasswordService class with hash(), verify(), validateStrength(), needsRehash(), and generateResetToken() methods. Write unit tests using Jest that verify: (1) the same password produces different hashes each time, (2) verify returns true for correct passwords and false for incorrect ones, (3) validateStrength rejects weak passwords.
exercises.hint: Use bcrypt.getRounds() to check the cost factor of an existing hash. For generateResetToken, use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex').

exercises.title: Migrate from SHA-256 to bcrypt
exercises.description: Simulate a legacy system migration. Start with an array of users whose passwords are stored as SHA-256 hashes. Write a login function that: (1) checks if the stored hash is SHA-256 (64 hex characters) or bcrypt (starts with $2b$), (2) verifies accordingly, (3) if SHA-256, rehashes with bcrypt and updates the stored hash. Write tests to confirm the migration works transparently.
exercises.hint: SHA-256 hashes are 64 hex characters. bcrypt hashes start with '$2b$'. After successful login with a SHA-256 hash, hash the plaintext password with bcrypt and update the user record.

exercises.title: Timing Attack Demonstration
exercises.description: Write two comparison functions: one using naive string comparison (===) and one using crypto.timingSafeEqual(). Create a test that compares strings with varying amounts of matching prefix characters. Measure and log the time for each comparison to observe whether the naive approach leaks timing information.
exercises.hint: Use process.hrtime.bigint() for nanosecond-precision timing. Compare strings like 'aaaaaaaaax' vs 'aaaaaaaaab' (9 matching chars) and 'axaaaaaaaa' vs 'abaaaaaaaa' (1 matching char). You may need to run thousands of iterations to see a measurable difference.

problems.problem: bcrypt.hash throws 'Error: data and salt arguments required' even though a password is provided
problems.cause: The password variable is undefined or null, often because req.body was not parsed (missing express.json() middleware) or the field name in the request does not match the destructured variable name.
problems.solution: Ensure app.use(express.json()) is registered before the route. Add a check: if (!password) return res.status(400)... before calling bcrypt.hash(). Log typeof password to debug.

problems.problem: bcrypt.compare always returns false even with the correct password
problems.cause: The stored hash was truncated when saved to the database. bcrypt hashes are 60 characters long, but the database column may be defined as VARCHAR(50) or similar, silently truncating the hash.
problems.solution: Ensure the password column in your database is at least VARCHAR(60) for bcrypt or VARCHAR(128) for argon2. Check the stored hash length: if it is less than 60 characters, the column is too short.

problems.problem: Installing the bcrypt package fails with node-gyp errors on macOS or Windows
problems.cause: The native bcrypt package requires C++ compilation tools (node-gyp, Python, make). These are not always installed on developer machines or CI environments.
problems.solution: Install build tools: on macOS run 'xcode-select --install', on Windows install windows-build-tools. Alternatively, switch to bcryptjs which is a pure JavaScript implementation: npm install bcryptjs. The API is identical.

7.3.2.intuition: Think of HMAC as a wax seal on a medieval letter: anyone can read the letter, but the seal proves it came from the king and has not been tampered with. Encryption is like putting the letter in a locked box -- only someone with the key can read it. AES-GCM gives you both the locked box and the wax seal in one operation.

exercises.title: Secure Token Generator
exercises.description: Build a TokenService class that generates three types of tokens: (1) email verification tokens (random hex, store SHA-256 hash in DB), (2) API keys with a 'sk_live_' prefix, and (3) short-lived numeric OTPs (6 digits). Write tests verifying that verification tokens validate correctly and that OTPs are always 6 digits.
exercises.hint: For email tokens, generate with randomBytes(32).toString('hex') and store createHash('sha256').update(token).digest('hex'). For OTPs, use randomInt(100000, 1000000) or randomBytes(4).readUInt32BE(0) % 1000000 with padding.

exercises.title: Webhook Signature Middleware
exercises.description: Create an Express middleware that verifies HMAC-SHA256 webhook signatures. The middleware should: read the x-webhook-signature header, compute the HMAC of the raw request body using a secret from environment variables, compare using timingSafeEqual, and reject with 401 if invalid. Write tests using supertest with both valid and tampered payloads.
exercises.hint: You need the raw body as a string for HMAC computation. Use express.json({ verify: (req, res, buf) => { req.rawBody = buf.toString(); }}) to capture it. The signature header is typically a hex-encoded HMAC.

exercises.title: Field-Level Encryption Service
exercises.description: Implement a FieldEncryptor class using AES-256-GCM that can encrypt and decrypt individual database fields (like SSN, credit card numbers). The encrypted output should be a single string containing the IV, ciphertext, and auth tag. Write tests that verify: encryption produces different output for the same input (due to random IV), decryption recovers the original value, and tampered ciphertext throws an error.
exercises.hint: Concatenate iv:ciphertext:authTag as hex strings separated by colons. For decryption, split on ':' to recover each component. createDecipheriv with a tampered auth tag will throw 'Unsupported state or unable to authenticate data'.

exercises.title: Signed URL Generator
exercises.description: Build a function that creates time-limited signed URLs for file downloads. The URL should include the file path, expiration timestamp, and an HMAC signature. Build a companion verification function that checks the signature and expiration. Test with valid URLs, expired URLs, and URLs with tampered parameters.
exercises.hint: Sign the string 'filepath:expires' with HMAC-SHA256. Append the signature and expiration as query parameters. On verification, reconstruct the string, recompute the HMAC, and check both the signature match and that Date.now() < expires.

problems.problem: createDecipheriv throws 'Unsupported state or unable to authenticate data'
problems.cause: The authentication tag, ciphertext, IV, or key does not match what was used during encryption. This commonly happens when the auth tag is not set before calling decipher.final(), or when the encrypted data was corrupted or truncated during storage.
problems.solution: Ensure you call decipher.setAuthTag(tag) before decipher.final(). Verify that the IV, ciphertext, and tag are correctly parsed from the stored string. Check that the same key is used for both encryption and decryption. If storing in a database, ensure the column is large enough to hold the full encrypted value.

problems.problem: createCipheriv throws 'Invalid key length' when using AES-256-GCM
problems.cause: AES-256 requires exactly 32 bytes (256 bits) for the key. The provided key is either too short or too long, often because a string was passed directly instead of a properly sized Buffer.
problems.solution: Generate keys with randomBytes(32) or derive them with scryptSync(password, salt, 32). If loading from an environment variable, decode it properly: Buffer.from(process.env.KEY, 'hex') for hex-encoded keys, or Buffer.from(process.env.KEY, 'base64') for base64-encoded keys.

problems.problem: HMAC verification always fails even though the message has not been tampered with
problems.cause: The message format differs between signing and verification. Common issues include JSON serialization producing different key orders, whitespace differences, or encoding mismatches (e.g., signing the raw body but verifying against JSON.stringify(req.body) which may differ).
problems.solution: Ensure byte-for-byte identical input to both HMAC computations. For webhooks, capture the raw request body with express.json({ verify: (req, res, buf) => req.rawBody = buf }) and use req.rawBody for HMAC computation instead of JSON.stringify(req.body).

7.3.3.intuition: A certificate is like a government-issued passport: the passport itself (certificate) contains your photo and name (public key and identity), and it is stamped by the government (Certificate Authority). When you cross a border, the guard verifies the stamp is legitimate. Mutual TLS is like a border where BOTH sides must show their passports before either is allowed through.

exercises.title: Generate and Inspect Certificates
exercises.description: Using OpenSSL, generate a self-signed CA certificate, then use that CA to sign a server certificate for localhost. Write a Node.js script that reads both certificates using the X509Certificate class and prints: subject, issuer, validity dates, days until expiration, and whether the server cert was issued by the CA.
exercises.hint: Use 'openssl req -x509' for the CA and 'openssl req -new' + 'openssl x509 -req -CA' for the server cert. In Node.js, use new X509Certificate(pem) and the checkIssued() method.

exercises.title: HTTPS Server with Express
exercises.description: Create an Express HTTPS server that uses the self-signed certificates you generated. Add a /health endpoint that returns the server's certificate fingerprint and expiration date. Test it with curl using the --cacert flag to trust your CA, and observe the error when you do not provide the CA.
exercises.hint: Use https.createServer({ key, cert }, app). With curl, use: curl --cacert ca-cert.pem https://localhost:8443/health. Without --cacert, curl will reject the self-signed cert.

exercises.title: Mutual TLS Service Communication
exercises.description: Set up a complete mTLS system: (1) create a CA, (2) issue a server cert and a client cert both signed by the CA, (3) create an Express mTLS server that requires client certificates, (4) create a client that presents its certificate when connecting. Add middleware that extracts the client's CN (Common Name) and logs it. Test that connections without a client certificate are rejected.
exercises.hint: Server options need requestCert: true and rejectUnauthorized: true. Use req.socket.getPeerCertificate() to read client cert details. For the client, use https.Agent with key, cert, and ca options.

problems.problem: HTTPS server fails with 'error:0906D06C:PEM routines:PEM_read_bio:no start line'
problems.cause: The certificate or key file is not in PEM format, is empty, or the file path is wrong. This also occurs when the key and cert files are swapped (key passed as cert or vice versa).
problems.solution: Verify the file begins with '-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----' (for certs) or '-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----' (for keys). Check that fs.readFileSync paths are correct and the files are readable. Ensure you are not passing the key file as the cert option.

problems.problem: mTLS client connection fails with 'UNABLE_TO_VERIFY_LEAF_SIGNATURE'
problems.cause: The client does not trust the CA that signed the server's certificate. The ca option is missing from the client's HTTPS agent, or the wrong CA certificate file was provided.
problems.solution: Pass the CA certificate that signed the server's cert in the client's ca option: new https.Agent({ ca: fs.readFileSync('ca-cert.pem'), key: ..., cert: ... }). If the server uses a different CA than the client certificates, both CAs must be included.

problems.problem: req.socket.getPeerCertificate() returns an empty object even though requestCert is true
problems.cause: The rejectUnauthorized option is set to false, which allows connections without valid client certificates. In this case, getPeerCertificate() returns an empty object for unauthenticated clients.
problems.solution: Set rejectUnauthorized: true to require valid client certificates. If you need to allow optional client certs, check if the returned object has a 'subject' property before accessing cert fields: if (cert && cert.subject) { ... }.

7.4.1.intuition: Zod is like a bouncer at a nightclub with a detailed guest list. It checks every person (piece of data) against the list (schema), and anyone who does not match the exact criteria gets turned away with a clear explanation of why. The best part is the bouncer also tells the staff inside exactly what type of guests to expect, so everyone is on the same page.

exercises.title: Build a Registration Validator
exercises.description: Create a Zod schema for user registration that validates: username (3-30 chars, alphanumeric and underscores only), email, password (min 8, must contain uppercase, lowercase, number, and special character), confirmPassword (must match password), dateOfBirth (must be at least 13 years old), and an optional phone number in E.164 format. Write tests that cover both valid and invalid inputs.
exercises.hint: Use .regex() for the username pattern, .superRefine() for cross-field password matching, and .refine() with a date comparison for age validation.

exercises.title: Express Validation Middleware
exercises.description: Build a reusable validation middleware factory that accepts Zod schemas for body, query, and params. It should return 400 with structured error messages on failure, and replace req.body/req.query/req.params with the parsed (and potentially transformed/coerced) data on success. Write a test suite using supertest.
exercises.hint: Use safeParse for each schema, collect all errors before responding, and make sure to handle coercion for query parameters (they arrive as strings).

exercises.title: API Schema Library
exercises.description: Create a complete set of Zod schemas for a blog API: Post (with title, content, slug, tags, status enum), Comment (with body, authorId), and pagination/filtering query schemas. Use schema composition (pick, omit, partial, extend) to derive CreatePost, UpdatePost, PostSummary, and PostListResponse schemas from the base Post schema.
exercises.hint: Start with a full PostSchema, then use .omit() for create (remove id, timestamps), .partial() for update, .pick() for summary, and z.object wrapping z.array() for list responses.

exercises.title: Recursive Schema Challenge
exercises.description: Create a Zod schema that validates a nested comment thread where each comment can have an array of child replies, and each reply can also have children (recursive). Limit the nesting depth to 5 levels. Validate a sample thread and infer the TypeScript type.
exercises.hint: Use z.lazy() to create recursive schemas. For depth limiting, you can nest z.object calls explicitly 5 levels deep or use a refinement that checks depth.

problems.problem: Query parameters fail number validation even though the value is correct
problems.cause: Express query parameters are always strings. `z.number()` expects an actual number type, so '5' fails validation.
problems.solution: Use `z.coerce.number()` instead of `z.number()` for query parameters. Coerce mode attempts to convert the value to the target type before validating: `z.coerce.number().int().positive()` will correctly parse the string '5' into the number 5.

problems.problem: TypeScript types do not update after changing the Zod schema
problems.cause: You are maintaining a separate TypeScript interface alongside the Zod schema instead of using z.infer. The two have drifted out of sync.
problems.solution: Delete the manual interface and use `type MyType = z.infer<typeof MySchema>` exclusively. This ensures the type always matches the schema. If you need to export types for other modules, export the inferred type.

problems.problem: Custom refinements do not show up in the correct field's errors
problems.cause: When using .refine() on an object schema, the error is added to the form-level errors, not to a specific field.
problems.solution: Use .superRefine() instead and call ctx.addIssue() with a specific path: `ctx.addIssue({ code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom, message: 'Passwords must match', path: ['confirmPassword'] })`. This places the error on the correct field.

7.4.2.intuition: If Zod is a bouncer with a typed guest list, Joi is an experienced customs officer who has been checking passports for years. The officer knows every edge case, can apply different rules based on your nationality (conditional validation with .when()), and speaks every language when explaining why you cannot enter (custom error messages). Joi may not predict your type like Zod, but its experience handling real-world complexity is unmatched.

exercises.title: Payment Form Validator
exercises.description: Build a Joi schema for a payment form that validates differently based on the payment method (credit_card, bank_transfer, crypto). For credit cards, require card number (Luhn check), expiry (MM/YY format, not expired), and CVV. For bank transfers, require account number and routing number. For crypto, require a wallet address. Use .when() for conditional fields and .forbidden() to prevent irrelevant fields.
exercises.hint: Use Joi.string().creditCard() for card validation. Use .when('method', { is: 'credit_card', then: Joi.required(), otherwise: Joi.forbidden() }) for conditional fields.

exercises.title: Express Validation Middleware with Joi
exercises.description: Create a validation middleware that uses Joi schemas for body, query, and params. It should collect all errors (abortEarly: false), strip unknown fields, and return a structured error response. Test with at least 5 different invalid inputs and verify the error format is consistent.
exercises.hint: Set options { abortEarly: false, stripUnknown: true, errors: { wrap: { label: false } } } and group error.details by path.

exercises.title: Configuration Validator
exercises.description: Create a Joi schema that validates your application's environment variables on startup. Include NODE_ENV, PORT, DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET (min 32 chars), JWT_EXPIRY (duration pattern), REDIS_URL, SMTP_HOST (required in production only), and LOG_LEVEL. If any required variable is missing, the app should print all errors and exit.
exercises.hint: Use Joi.when() referencing NODE_ENV for production-only requirements. Use Joi.string().uri({ scheme: ['postgres', 'postgresql'] }) for DATABASE_URL.

exercises.title: Joi vs Zod Comparison
exercises.description: Implement the same validation schema in both Joi and Zod: a complex order schema with items array (each with product ID, quantity, price), shipping address (with conditional fields based on country), and discount code (optional, specific format). Compare the developer experience, error output, and TypeScript integration.
exercises.hint: Focus on conditional validation (.when() in Joi vs .refine()/.superRefine() in Zod) and how each handles type inference.

problems.problem: Joi strips fields that were expected in the handler
problems.cause: Using { stripUnknown: true } removes any field not defined in the schema. If you forgot to include a field in the schema, it silently disappears.
problems.solution: Double-check that your schema includes all expected fields. During development, use { allowUnknown: true } first, then switch to { stripUnknown: true } once the schema is complete. Log the validated value to verify all fields are present.

problems.problem: Only the first validation error is returned to the client
problems.cause: Joi's default behavior is abortEarly: true, which stops validation at the first error.
problems.solution: Pass { abortEarly: false } as a validation option: `schema.validate(input, { abortEarly: false })`. This collects all validation errors so the client can fix everything in one attempt.

problems.problem: Joi error messages include ugly quotes and label prefixes
problems.cause: By default, Joi wraps field names in quotes in error messages, producing output like '"name" is required'.
problems.solution: Set errors.wrap.label to false in options: `schema.validate(input, { errors: { wrap: { label: false } } })`. For even cleaner messages, use the .messages() method on each field to provide fully custom error text.

7.4.3.intuition: Think of file upload validation like airport security. The client-provided filename and Content-Type are like the label on your suitcase -- anyone can write anything on it. The real security comes from opening the suitcase (reading magic bytes), running it through the X-ray machine (content validation with sharp/file-type), and checking for prohibited items (malware, scripts). Just like airports do not let you carry your bag directly to the plane, you should never let uploaded files land directly in a publicly accessible directory.

exercises.title: Secure Avatar Upload Endpoint
exercises.description: Build a complete avatar upload endpoint with Express and Multer. It should: accept only JPEG/PNG/WebP, limit to 5MB, validate magic bytes with file-type, re-encode through sharp to 400x400 pixels, generate a random filename, store in a non-public directory, and return the access URL. Include error handling for every failure mode.
exercises.hint: Use multer's memoryStorage to get a buffer, validate with fileTypeFromBuffer(), process with sharp().resize().toFile(), and serve through a separate GET endpoint with proper headers.

exercises.title: Multi-File Upload with Validation
exercises.description: Create an endpoint that accepts up to 10 files of mixed types (images and PDFs). Validate each file independently (images: magic bytes + sharp processing; PDFs: magic bytes + size check). Return a response showing which files succeeded and which were rejected, with reasons for each rejection.
exercises.hint: Use upload.array('files', 10) and process each file in a Promise.allSettled() call to handle partial failures gracefully.

exercises.title: Malicious Upload Test Suite
exercises.description: Write tests that attempt to bypass your upload validation: rename a PHP file to .jpg, create a file with JPEG magic bytes followed by PHP code, upload a file with path traversal in the name (../../etc/passwd.jpg), upload a 0-byte file, and upload a file exceeding the size limit. Verify each attack is properly blocked.
exercises.hint: Use Node.js Buffer to craft files with specific magic bytes. Use supertest to send multipart requests. Verify files are rejected and temp files are cleaned up.

problems.problem: Uploaded images are served with wrong Content-Type, causing the browser to download instead of display
problems.cause: The serving endpoint is not setting the Content-Type header based on the actual file type, or it is always setting Content-Disposition: attachment.
problems.solution: When serving images for display (like avatars), use Content-Disposition: inline and set the correct Content-Type based on the stored MIME type. For downloads, use Content-Disposition: attachment. Always set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff to prevent MIME sniffing.

problems.problem: Temp files accumulate and fill up disk space
problems.cause: When upload processing fails (validation error, sharp crash, S3 timeout), the temporary file is not cleaned up.
problems.solution: Always wrap upload processing in try/catch/finally and delete the temp file in the finally block. Additionally, set up a cron job or periodic cleanup that removes files older than 1 hour from the temp directory: `find /var/uploads/tmp -mmin +60 -delete`.

problems.problem: CORS errors when uploading from the frontend
problems.cause: The browser sends a preflight OPTIONS request for multipart uploads, and the server does not handle it.
problems.solution: Configure CORS middleware before your upload routes: `app.use(cors({ origin: 'https://yourfrontend.com', methods: ['POST'], allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization'] }))`. Make sure the CORS middleware handles OPTIONS requests.

7.5.1.intuition: Winston is like a sophisticated mail sorting facility. Every log message is a letter that arrives at the facility with a priority stamp (log level). The facility has multiple outbound trucks (transports) -- one for the console display, one for daily file archives, one for the error department. Each truck only accepts letters above a certain priority. The formatting machine stamps every letter with a timestamp and return address (metadata) before it goes out, ensuring every destination gets consistently formatted mail.

exercises.title: Production Logger Module
exercises.description: Create a reusable logger module that configures Winston with: JSON format in production, pretty-print in development, daily file rotation, separate error log file, request ID tracking, and sensitive data redaction. Export both the logger and a middleware function for Express. Write tests that verify log output format and redaction.
exercises.hint: Use winston.format.combine() to chain formats. Use a child logger per request with requestId. Test by creating a custom transport that captures log entries into an array.

exercises.title: HTTP Request Logger Middleware
exercises.description: Build Express middleware that logs every request and response with: method, URL, status code, response time in ms, content length, user agent, and IP address. Log 5xx responses at error level, 4xx at warn level, and 2xx/3xx at info level. Include a request ID that is returned in the response headers.
exercises.hint: Listen to the 'finish' event on the response object to capture the status code and timing. Use process.hrtime.bigint() for precise timing.

exercises.title: Log Aggregation Simulation
exercises.description: Create a script that generates 1000 structured log entries across multiple simulated services (auth, orders, payments) with realistic patterns (mostly info, some warnings, occasional errors). Write a second script that reads the log file and produces a summary report: error count by service, average response time, top 5 error messages, and requests per minute.
exercises.hint: Write logs as newline-delimited JSON. Parse with readline and JSON.parse. Use Map objects to aggregate statistics.

problems.problem: Winston logs are not appearing in the console
problems.cause: The log level is set higher than the level being logged. For example, level is 'error' but you are calling logger.info().
problems.solution: Check the logger's level setting. Winston only outputs messages at or above the configured level. Set level to 'debug' during development: `winston.createLogger({ level: 'debug' })`. You can also set level per-transport.

problems.problem: Stack traces are missing from error logs
problems.cause: Winston does not automatically serialize Error objects' stack traces. Passing an Error as metadata without the errors format loses the stack.
problems.solution: Add `winston.format.errors({ stack: true })` to your format chain, and make sure it comes BEFORE the json() or printf() format. Then pass errors as: `logger.error('Failed', { error: err })` or `logger.error(err)`.

problems.problem: Log files grow indefinitely and fill up disk space
problems.cause: Using the basic File transport without rotation or size limits.
problems.solution: Install and use winston-daily-rotate-file: `new DailyRotateFile({ maxSize: '20m', maxFiles: '14d', zippedArchive: true })`. This rotates logs daily, compresses old files, and automatically deletes files older than the retention period.

7.5.2.intuition: If Winston is a full-service mail sorting facility, Pino is like a high-speed conveyor belt with a barcode printer. It slaps a barcode (JSON) on every package (log entry) with minimal handling and immediately sends it down the belt to the destination. The speed comes from doing less work in the critical path -- no fancy formatting, no multiple stops, just structured data out the door as fast as possible. Processing and pretty-printing happen downstream, not in your app's hot path.

exercises.title: Pino Logger Setup
exercises.description: Create a Pino-based logger module for an Express application. Configure it with: pretty-printing in development (pino-pretty), JSON in production, request ID tracking, custom serializers for req/res/err, redaction of authorization headers and password fields, and a custom log level function that logs 5xx as error and 4xx as warn. Write a test that validates the JSON output structure.
exercises.hint: Use pino-http for Express integration. Set transport conditionally based on NODE_ENV. Use pino's built-in redact option with path patterns.

exercises.title: Pino vs Winston Benchmark
exercises.description: Write a benchmark script that compares Pino and Winston performance: create both loggers writing to files, generate 100,000 log entries with each, and measure total time and event loop delay. Include scenarios for simple messages, messages with metadata objects, and messages with error objects.
exercises.hint: Use process.hrtime.bigint() for timing. Use perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay() to measure event loop impact. Write to /dev/null or a temp file to eliminate disk I/O as a variable.

exercises.title: Log Pipeline Simulation
exercises.description: Build a complete logging pipeline: a Pino logger writes NDJSON to a file, a separate script reads the file using readline, parses each line, and pushes entries to different destinations based on level (errors to a Slack webhook simulation, all logs to a summary report). Demonstrate how structured logging enables automated processing.
exercises.hint: Use pino file transport to write logs. Use Node.js readline to read NDJSON. Use a Map to aggregate counts by level. Simulate Slack by writing to a separate 'alerts.json' file.

problems.problem: Pino logs show numeric levels instead of level names
problems.cause: Pino uses numeric levels in JSON output for performance. This is by design, but it can be confusing when reading raw logs.
problems.solution: For development, use pino-pretty which translates levels to names. For production, your log aggregation tool (ELK, Datadog) should map the numeric levels. If you absolutely need string levels in JSON, use the `formatters` option: `formatters: { level: (label) => ({ level: label }) }`.

problems.problem: Logs are lost on process crash or SIGTERM
problems.cause: Pino's async transports buffer log entries. On abrupt termination, buffered entries may not be flushed to disk.
problems.solution: Use `pino.final()` to create a special logger for shutdown scenarios. Register handlers for 'uncaughtException', 'unhandledRejection', 'SIGTERM', and 'SIGINT' that call the final logger. For synchronous flushing, use `pino.destination({ sync: true })` but note this reduces performance.

problems.problem: pino-pretty slows down the application significantly
problems.cause: pino-pretty is doing formatting work that adds overhead. Using it in production defeats Pino's performance advantage.
problems.solution: Only use pino-pretty in development. In production, output raw JSON and let your log aggregation tool handle formatting. Set the transport conditionally: `transport: process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production' ? { target: 'pino-pretty' } : undefined`.

7.5.3.intuition: Prometheus metrics are like the dashboard instruments in a car. The speedometer (gauge) shows your current speed, the odometer (counter) tracks total distance traveled and only goes up, and the trip computer (histogram) tells you the distribution of your speeds over time -- average, fastest, slowest. Just as you would not drive a car without a dashboard, you should not run a production service without metrics. The /metrics endpoint is your dashboard, Prometheus is the computer recording everything, and Grafana is the fancy display screen.

exercises.title: RED Metrics Middleware
exercises.description: Build an Express middleware that implements the RED method using prom-client: a counter for total requests (labeled by method, route, status_code), a histogram for request duration (same labels), and derive the error rate from the counter. Expose a /metrics endpoint. Create a load test script using a loop of fetch() calls to generate traffic, then verify the metrics output.
exercises.hint: Use histogram.startTimer() which returns a function that records the duration when called. Use route patterns (req.route?.path) instead of full URLs to keep label cardinality low.

exercises.title: Custom Business Metrics Dashboard
exercises.description: Add business-level metrics to a sample e-commerce API: orders placed (counter with payment method label), order value (histogram with dollar amount buckets), active shopping carts (gauge), and cache hit ratio (gauge). Write a script that simulates traffic and generates realistic metric data. Document the PromQL queries you would use in Grafana for each metric.
exercises.hint: For the cache hit ratio, maintain two counters (hits and total) and calculate the ratio in PromQL: rate(cache_hits[5m]) / rate(cache_total[5m]).

exercises.title: Prometheus + Grafana Stack
exercises.description: Set up a complete monitoring stack with Docker Compose: your Node.js app with prom-client, Prometheus scraping the /metrics endpoint, and Grafana with a pre-configured dashboard. The dashboard should show: request rate, error rate percentage, p50/p95/p99 latency, memory usage, event loop lag, and active connections. Export the Grafana dashboard JSON.
exercises.hint: Use Grafana's provisioning to auto-configure the Prometheus data source. Use histogram_quantile() for latency percentiles. Set appropriate time ranges (5m for rates, 1m for gauges).

exercises.title: Alerting Rules
exercises.description: Write Prometheus alerting rules for: error rate above 5% for 5 minutes, p99 latency above 2 seconds for 10 minutes, memory usage above 80% of limit, and event loop lag above 100ms. Configure these in a prometheus rules file and test them by intentionally degrading your application.
exercises.hint: Use recording rules to pre-compute expensive PromQL. Alert rules use the `for` clause to avoid flapping. Use `labels` and `annotations` for alert metadata.

problems.problem: Prometheus scrape target shows as DOWN in the targets page
problems.cause: Prometheus cannot reach the /metrics endpoint. Common reasons: wrong hostname/port in prometheus.yml, Docker networking issues (using localhost instead of container name), or the app is not exposing /metrics.
problems.solution: Verify the app serves /metrics by curling it directly: `curl http://localhost:3000/metrics`. In Docker Compose, use the service name as the hostname (e.g., 'app:3000' not 'localhost:3000'). Check Prometheus logs for connection errors.

problems.problem: High cardinality causing Prometheus to run out of memory
problems.cause: Labels contain high-cardinality values like user IDs, request IDs, or full URLs with path parameters (e.g., /api/users/abc123 instead of /api/users/:id).
problems.solution: Replace dynamic path segments with route patterns: use req.route?.path (e.g., '/api/users/:id') instead of req.originalUrl. Never use user IDs, session IDs, or timestamps as label values. Monitor Prometheus' own metrics: `prometheus_tsdb_head_series` shows total time series count.

problems.problem: Histogram percentiles are inaccurate or show unexpected values
problems.cause: The histogram bucket boundaries do not cover the actual range of observed values. If most requests take 50-200ms but your smallest bucket is 1s, all observations land in the same bucket.
problems.solution: Choose bucket boundaries that match your expected distribution. For HTTP latency: [0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10]. Use histogram_quantile() with rate() for accurate percentile calculation: `histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))`.

7.6.1.intuition: A circuit breaker works like the electrical breaker box in your house. When a short circuit (failed service) causes too much current (failed requests), the breaker trips and cuts the connection instantly, protecting the entire house (your application) from catching fire (cascading failure). After a cooldown, you can flip the switch to test if the problem is fixed.

exercises.title: Build a Circuit Breaker from Scratch
exercises.description: Implement a simple CircuitBreaker class in Node.js with three states (CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN). It should accept a threshold count and reset timeout. Track failure counts, transition states, and provide a fire() method that executes the wrapped function or throws when the circuit is open. Write tests that simulate failures and verify state transitions.
exercises.hint: Use an internal state variable and counters. In CLOSED state, count failures and trip to OPEN when threshold is exceeded. Use setTimeout to transition from OPEN to HALF_OPEN. In HALF_OPEN, allow one request through and close the circuit on success or reopen on failure.

exercises.title: Opossum with Express Middleware
exercises.description: Create an Express middleware factory that wraps any async route handler function with an opossum circuit breaker. The middleware should return a 503 with Retry-After header when the circuit is open, log state transitions using pino, and expose a /health/circuits endpoint that lists all breaker states.
exercises.hint: Store breakers in a Map keyed by name. The factory function takes a name, the async function, and options. Return an Express middleware (req, res, next) that calls breaker.fire(). Use breaker.opened, breaker.closed properties to report state.

exercises.title: Exponential Backoff with Jitter Visualization
exercises.description: Write a script that simulates 100 clients retrying simultaneously. Compare three strategies: (1) fixed delay, (2) exponential backoff without jitter, (3) exponential backoff with jitter. For each strategy, log the total number of requests hitting the server per second over a 30-second window. Observe how jitter spreads the load.
exercises.hint: Use an array to track request timestamps. For each simulated client, calculate when each retry would occur. Use full jitter: delay = random(0, baseDelay * 2^attempt). Bucket the timestamps into 1-second intervals and print a histogram.

exercises.title: Retry Budget Implementation
exercises.description: Implement a RetryBudget class that tracks the ratio of retries to total requests over a sliding time window. Only allow a retry if retries are below 10% of total requests. Integrate it with the async-retry library. Write tests that verify the budget is enforced under high failure rates.
exercises.hint: Use two arrays of timestamps (requests and retries). Clean up entries older than the window on each check. The canRetry() method returns true only when retries.length / requests.length < maxRetryRatio.

problems.problem: Circuit breaker never closes after the downstream service recovers
problems.cause: The resetTimeout is too long, or the half-open probe requests are failing because the service returns errors on the first request after recovery (e.g., cold start). Another common cause is that the health check endpoint recovers but the actual operation endpoint has not.
problems.solution: Reduce the resetTimeout to test recovery sooner (e.g., 10-15 seconds instead of 60). Ensure the half-open probe hits a lightweight operation. Monitor the breaker state with events and metrics. In opossum, verify your volumeThreshold is not too high for low-traffic services.

problems.problem: Retry storms overload a recovering service and cause it to fail again
problems.cause: All clients retry simultaneously after their backoff timers expire, or jitter is not implemented. A service that was just recovering gets hit with a spike of retry traffic and goes down again.
problems.solution: Always use jitter with exponential backoff. Implement a retry budget to cap total retry traffic at 10-20% of normal request volume. Combine retries with a circuit breaker so that when failures reach a threshold, retries stop entirely. Consider adding server-side rate limiting with Retry-After headers.

problems.problem: Non-idempotent operations produce duplicate side effects on retry
problems.cause: Retrying a POST request that creates a resource (order, payment, email) without an idempotency key causes the operation to execute multiple times.
problems.solution: Generate a unique idempotency key on the client side and include it in the request header. The server must check this key before processing: if the key was already seen, return the cached response. Store idempotency keys in Redis with a TTL. Only retry operations that are naturally idempotent (GET, PUT, DELETE) without extra safeguards.

7.6.2.intuition: Think of graceful degradation like a restaurant during a power outage. The kitchen gas stove still works, so they can serve hot food, but the blender is down so no smoothies. They tell customers 'limited menu tonight' rather than closing entirely. Health checks are like the health inspector: liveness checks that the building exists, and readiness checks that the kitchen is clean and stocked.

exercises.title: Product Page with Graceful Degradation
exercises.description: Build an Express API endpoint for /products/:id that aggregates data from three sources: a product database (critical), a recommendation engine (non-critical), and a review service (non-critical). Use opossum circuit breakers with fallbacks for non-critical services. If the recommendation service is down, return an empty list. If the review service is down, return cached reviews. Include a _degraded field in the response indicating which services are degraded.
exercises.hint: Use Promise.allSettled() to fetch non-critical data concurrently. Wrap each external call in its own circuit breaker with a fallback function. Check each settled promise's status to populate the _degraded field.

exercises.title: Terminus Health Check Integration
exercises.description: Create a complete Express application with @godaddy/terminus. Implement separate liveness (/healthz) and readiness (/readyz) endpoints. The liveness endpoint should return 200 with no external checks. The readiness endpoint should verify Prisma database connectivity and Redis connectivity, returning detailed status for each. Implement proper graceful shutdown that disconnects both.
exercises.hint: Pass separate functions to terminus healthChecks object. The liveness function just resolves. The readiness function runs prisma.$queryRaw and redis.ping() in parallel with Promise.all. Remember to implement onSignal for cleanup.

exercises.title: Load Shedding Middleware
exercises.description: Implement an Express middleware that tracks active in-flight requests. When the count exceeds a configurable threshold (e.g., 200), reject new requests with HTTP 503 and a Retry-After header. Add a priority system: requests with an X-Priority: high header bypass the limit (up to a separate, higher cap). Write tests that verify shedding behavior.
exercises.hint: Increment a counter on request entry and decrement on the 'finish' event of the response. Check the counter in the middleware before calling next(). For priority, maintain two counters: one for normal traffic and one for high-priority.

problems.problem: Liveness probe depends on the database and causes restart loops
problems.cause: The liveness health check queries the database. When the database goes down, the liveness probe fails, Kubernetes restarts the pod, the new pod also cannot reach the database, the probe fails again, and the pod enters CrashLoopBackOff.
problems.solution: Separate liveness from readiness. Liveness probes should only verify the process is running (return 200 with no external calls). Readiness probes check dependencies. When the database is down, the readiness probe fails and the pod is removed from the load balancer, but it is not restarted. Once the database recovers, the readiness probe passes and the pod is added back.

problems.problem: Graceful shutdown is interrupted, leaving database connections open
problems.cause: The SIGTERM handler does not wait for in-flight requests to complete before closing connections, or there is no forced shutdown timeout so the process hangs indefinitely waiting for a stuck connection.
problems.solution: Use @godaddy/terminus or implement a two-phase shutdown: first stop accepting new connections (server.close()), then wait for in-flight requests to drain, then close database and Redis connections. Always set a maximum timeout (e.g., 30 seconds) and force process.exit(1) if cleanup has not finished.

problems.problem: Health check endpoint exposes sensitive internal information
problems.cause: The health check returns detailed error messages, stack traces, or internal service URLs that could aid an attacker.
problems.solution: Return detailed health information only for internal health check paths (e.g., behind a VPC or protected by auth). Public-facing health endpoints should return minimal information: just the status code (200 or 503) and a simple status string. Use different endpoints for different audiences: /healthz (public, minimal) and /internal/health (detailed, internal only).
