7.6.3.intuition: Chaos engineering is like a fire drill for your servers. You do not wait for a real fire to find out that the emergency exit is blocked and the fire extinguisher is expired. By running controlled 'fires' regularly, you discover weaknesses while everyone is awake, alert, and ready to respond -- not at 3 AM when the real outage hits.

exercises.title: Build a Chaos HTTP Interceptor
exercises.description: Create a configurable axios interceptor that can inject three types of chaos: latency (configurable delay), failure (configurable error rate and status code), and corruption (modify response data randomly). Build a REST API to enable/disable each chaos type at runtime. Only activate the interceptor when CHAOS_ENABLED=true. Test it against a simple Express app and observe how circuit breakers react.
exercises.hint: Use axios.interceptors.request.use for latency and failure injection, and axios.interceptors.response.use for response corruption. Store experiment configs in a Map and expose POST endpoints to toggle them.

exercises.title: Chaos Experiment with Toxiproxy
exercises.description: Set up a Docker Compose environment with your Express app, PostgreSQL, and Redis all routing through Toxiproxy. Write a test suite that: (1) adds 2s latency to PostgreSQL and verifies the app still responds, (2) kills the Redis connection and verifies the app degrades gracefully, (3) adds 30% packet loss to PostgreSQL and measures the increase in p99 latency.
exercises.hint: Use the toxiproxy-node-client package to programmatically add and remove toxics. Use the Toxiproxy REST API on port 8474. Create proxies that match your database and Redis ports.

exercises.title: Event Loop Lag Monitor and Experiment
exercises.description: Build an event loop lag monitoring module using setTimeout drift detection and the perf_hooks monitorEventLoopDelay API. Then create a chaos experiment that intentionally blocks the event loop for configurable durations. Run the experiment and verify: (1) the monitor detects the lag, (2) health checks report degraded state, (3) the application recovers after the experiment ends.
exercises.hint: Use perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay() to get a histogram of event loop delays. Compare with a manual approach using setInterval and checking actual vs expected elapsed time. Expose lag metrics on your /metrics endpoint.

exercises.title: Automated Chaos Test Suite
exercises.description: Write a Jest test suite that runs against your staging environment and performs three chaos experiments: downstream service failure, high latency, and connection pool exhaustion. Each test should verify the application maintains its SLO (p99 < 2s, error rate < 5%). Include setup/teardown to enable/disable experiments and a global abort mechanism if error rate exceeds a safety threshold.
exercises.hint: Use beforeAll to enable chaos and afterAll to disable it. Poll the /metrics endpoint during the test to check error rates. Implement a custom Jest reporter or use setInterval with expect() assertions.

problems.problem: Chaos experiment caused a real production outage
problems.cause: The blast radius was not limited. The experiment affected all instances/all traffic instead of a small subset. There was no kill switch or the kill switch was too slow.
problems.solution: Always start with the smallest possible blast radius: one pod, one percent of traffic, one region. Implement an instant kill switch (a feature flag that disables all experiments). Set automatic abort criteria based on metrics thresholds (e.g., abort if error rate > 5%). Never run chaos experiments without monitoring dashboards open and team members on standby.

problems.problem: Chaos experiments pass in staging but failures still happen in production
problems.cause: Staging does not replicate production conditions: lower traffic volume, different data sizes, no real user behavior patterns, different infrastructure configuration, missing edge cases that only appear at scale.
problems.solution: Gradually move chaos experiments to production with strict blast radius controls. Use traffic mirroring to test with production-like traffic in staging. Ensure staging infrastructure matches production as closely as possible (same database version, same connection pool sizes, same timeouts). Focus on experiments that test the specific differences between staging and production.

problems.problem: Team resistance to chaos engineering -- 'Why would we break things on purpose?'
problems.cause: Lack of understanding of the purpose, fear of causing outages, no executive sponsorship, or no clear process for running experiments safely.
problems.solution: Start with non-production environments to build confidence. Run a Game Day with the team to demonstrate value. Show examples of real outages that chaos engineering would have prevented. Establish clear safety protocols (blast radius, kill switch, abort criteria). Get buy-in from leadership by framing chaos engineering as risk reduction, not recklessness.
